title: Dissipative acousto-mechanical parametric interface between high-overtone acoustics and flexural phonons
authors: Xun Ji, Huanying Sun, Longhao Wu, Qichun Liu, Yulong Liu, Mika A. Sillanpää, Tiefu Li
abstract: High-overtone bulk acoustic wave resonators (HBARs) promise advanced phononics, yet achieving nonlinearity remains challenging. We demonstrate a radiation-pressure-type parametric interaction between GHz HBARs and low-frequency flexural modes in a suspended silicon nitride membrane, where mechanical displacement modulates the external dissipation rate to enable dissipative acousto-mechanical coupling. Benefiting from the high quality factor, the system enters the resolved-sideband regime at room temperature, yielding acousto-mechanically induced transparency. We observe tunable Kerr nonlinearity and generate coherent HBAR frequency combs via two-tone driving. Notably, our dissipative coupling strength is 20 times larger than the dispersive coupling, the highest ratio among reported hybrid dissipative-dispersive coupling systems, resulting in the experimental observation of amplification in the reflection spectra under red-sideband driving. The ability to interface dense HBAR modes with a common mechanical resonator provides a scalable on-chip platform for multimode phononic information processing, with quantum phononics potentially achievable at sub-Kelvin temperatures.
summary: The researchers created a hybrid device that links high-frequency acoustic waves to low-frequency vibrations using a dissipative coupling mechanism. This setup allows for the generation of acoustic frequency combs and transparency windows at room temperature. It provides a scalable platform for future phononic information processing and quantum acoustics.
main_problem: Achieving efficient nonlinearity and parametric interaction between high-frequency GHz HBAR modes and low-frequency sub-MHz flexural membrane modes for scalable phononic information processing.
main_result: Demonstration of a dissipative-dominated acousto-mechanical interface where the dissipative coupling strength is 20 times larger than the dispersive coupling, enabling acoustic frequency combs and induced transparency.
method: A flip-chip assembly using microwave reflection spectroscopy (|S11|), two-tone driving, and time-domain analysis of I/Q components to observe nonlinear acoustic phenomena.
model_or_system: A hybrid architecture integrating a high-overtone bulk acoustic wave resonator (HBAR) on sapphire with a low-frequency SiN membrane. The system is modeled using a modified Butterworth-Van Dyke circuit and a system-bath interaction Hamiltonian.
key_observables: Acousto-mechanically induced transparency (AMIT), acousto-mechanically induced amplification (AMIA), Kerr nonlinearity, and acoustic frequency combs.
important_parameters: HBAR frequency ~2.74 GHz, membrane frequency ~571.5 kHz, dissipative-to-dispersive coupling ratio ~20, and resolved-sideband regime at room temperature.
main_assumptions: The system operates in the resolved-sideband regime and assumes the mechanical displacement primarily modulates the external dissipation rate.
figures_summary: The notes mention Figure 1 and Figure 4(d), which likely detail the device architecture and the verification of the modulation period matching the mechanical cycle.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the challenge of HBAR nonlinearity, describes the hybrid HBAR-membrane platform, presents the mathematical model for dissipative coupling, details the experimental observation of AMIT and frequency combs, and discusses scalability for quantum phononics.
why_interesting: It demonstrates a high-ratio dissipative coupling regime and room-temperature nonlinear phonon dynamics, which are highly relevant for developing scalable, on-chip quantum-classical interfaces and many-body phononic networks.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. (a) Schematic of the dissipative acousto-mechanical interface (not to scale), featuring a high-overtone bulk acoustic resonator (HBAR) and a SiN membrane mechanical resonator integrated via flip-chip bonding. (b) Conceptual illustration of the coupling: the flexural dis- placement of the membrane modulates the HBAR’s external dissipation rate κex. (c) Schematic of the probed reflection spectra |S11| for varying membrane displacements xi (x0 < x1 < x2). The varying attenuation directly manifests the dissipative coupling. (d) Equivalent lumped-element circuit and experimental configuration. All measurements are conducted at room temperature in a high-vacuum chamber. PSG: multi-channel...

FIG. 2. (a) Microwave reflection spectra |S11| of selected HBAR modes. The inset shows a zoomed-in view of an acoustic mode along with its Lorentzian fit. (b) Ringdown measurement of the fundamen- tal flexural mechanical mode of the SiN membrane.

FIG. 3. (a) Reflection spectra |S11| of the AMIT window (color scale) as a function of red-detuned pump power. (b) Detailed view of the transition region from (a) for selected drive powers with the fitted AMIT (red lines). (c)–(e) Measured transparency window character- istics at a pump power of 3 dBm. (c) the transmission amplitude of the AMIT peak, (d) the accompanying steep phase dispersion, and (e) the resulting group delay τg calculated from the phase derivative.

FIG. 4. (a) Schematics of the two-tone pumping configuration, consisting of a resonant drive (P1) and a red-sideband drive (P2 at ∆≈−ωm). (b) Evolution of the AFC spectra measured by RSA under varying pump powers, with P1 = 15 dBm fixed and P2 varied. Controllable tooth counts and equidistant sidebands are observed. (c) Time-domain trace of the in-phase component, exhibiting clear periodic oscillations with δt = 1.75 µs. (d) Lissajous figure reconstructed from the IQ amplitudes, where the closed and stable orbits verify the long-range phase coherence of the generated frequency combs.
